test_objective: Capture experience with fragmented rural care and limited digital access

turns.text:
Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Thanks — I’ll ask some follow‑up questions to get more detail. Feel free to answer as much or as little as you want, and tell me if you’d rather skip any question.

Questions about expectations and initial steps
- When you imagined getting help, who did you picture going to first — a GP/primary care doctor, an OB/GYN, a midwife, a fertility clinic, or someone else?
  - Can you say why you expected that?
- What kind of help did you expect to get right away (e.g., information about timing/ovulation, tests, lifestyle advice, referrals)?
  - Can you give an example of a specific piece of information or action you thought would happen?
- How soon did you expect to be seen or to start tests/treatment once you asked for help?

Questions about what actually happened
- What was the first thing that actually happened when you sought support? Who did you talk to and how (phone, in‑person, email, online portal)?
  - How long did it take to get that first contact or appointment?
- What kinds of information, tests, or referrals did you actually receive?
  - Were any promised tests or referrals delayed or not provided at all?
- How did the care you received compare with what you had expected? In what ways was it better or worse?

Questions about communication and responsiveness
- How easy or hard was it to get clear, understandable information from the professionals you saw?
  - Can you remember an example of a time information was confusing or especially helpful?
- How responsive were providers to your calls, messages, or questions?
  - If you used an online patient portal or email, did you find replies timely and useful?
- Did you feel listened to and taken seriously? Were there moments you felt dismissed or rushed?
  - Can you describe one of those moments?

Questions about emotional support and shared decision‑making
- Did the healthcare professionals offer emotional support or counselling, or refer you to support groups?
  - Was this helpful or not?
- Were you involved in decisions about tests or treatments? Did you feel you had enough information to make choices?
  - If not, what was missing?

Questions about access, barriers, and practical issues
- Were there practical barriers (cost, insurance, travel, time off work, childcare) that affected your ability to get the support you wanted?
  - Which was the biggest barrier for you?
- Did language, cultural, religious, or other personal needs get addressed appropriately?
  - If not, how did that affect your care?

Questions about coordination of care
- If you saw multiple providers (e.g., GP and fertility specialist), how well did they communicate and coordinate your care?
  - Were there gaps, repeated tests, or conflicting advice?
- Were referrals made when needed? Were they timely?

Questions about outcomes and satisfaction
- Overall, how satisfied were you with the support you received during the process of trying to conceive?
  - What worked well and what didn’t?
- Did the support you received affect your decisions about continuing, pausing, or changing plans for conceiving? How?

Questions about partner and family involvement
- Were your partner or family included in appointments or discussions if you wanted them to be?
  - How did their involvement (or lack of it) affect your experience?

Questions for those who used fertility services or advanced treatments
- If you used fertility clinics, what was different about the support there compared with primary care?
  - Were clinic staff (nurses, coordinators) helpful and accessible?
- Were risks, success rates, and costs explained clearly before treatments?

Closing and suggestions
- Looking back, what would “ideal” support have looked like for you from start to finish?
  - What’s the single most important change you would recommend to healthcare professionals or clinics?
- Is there anything else about your experience with healthcare support while trying to conceive that you want to tell me but I haven’t asked about?

Optional probes I’ll use if answers are brief
- Can you say more about that?
- Can you give a specific example?
- How did that make you feel?
- When did that happen? (approximate timing)
- Who said that or did that?

Thank you — you can answer any of the above in any order. If you want, we can start with your first contact with a provider and walk through it step by step.

Main gentle probe (natural phrasing)
- "When you first talked with a healthcare provider about trying to get pregnant, what surprised you most — whether it was a good surprise or a bad one?"

Simpler / lower-literacy version
- "When you first spoke with a doctor or nurse about trying to have a baby, what surprised you the most — something good or something not so good?"

Short follow-up probes to use if their answer is brief
- "Can you tell me more about that?"
- "What exactly happened that surprised you?"
- "How did that make you feel?"

Probes to explore power dynamics (use after they mention who made decisions, how they were treated, or if they felt rushed)
- "Did you feel like the provider took your wishes into account, or were decisions made for you?"
- "Can you give an example of a time they made a choice without asking you? What was that like?"
- "Did you feel comfortable asking questions or pushing back if you disagreed?"

Probes to explore trust/distrust (use when they mention confidence, doubts, or credibility)
- "Did you feel you could trust the provider's advice at first? Why or why not?"
- "Was there anything that made you trust them more — or made you lose trust?"
- "Did you get the sense they understood your situation and concerns?"

Brief interviewer guidance (tone and pacing)
- Ask with a neutral, nonjudgmental tone and give pauses so the participant can think.
- If they seem uncomfortable, add: "It's okay if you don't want to go into details."
- Avoid leading language (don’t imply judgment about the provider or the patient).
- Use the short follow-ups to gently draw out concrete examples and feelings.

That there wasn’t one person who actually knew me. Felt like I was shuttled between a doc who showed up once a month, a nurse on a spotty satellite, and whatever ER folks were on duty. Records didn’t follow me — nobody had the whole picture.

Good surprise: the traveling OB usually showed up when he said he would and tried to help. Bad surprise: telehealth kept dropping out and they’d tell me to “come in” like I could just hop in town — 90 miles to the hospital — that don’t fly out here. And I didn’t expect the high blood pressure to sneak up on me — preeclampsia hit quicker than I figured.

Made me feel worried and a mite mad. Like I was doing half the job myself. Also some stuff they suggested — classes, group visits — don’t apply out here.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you imagine your emotional readiness would feel like — for example, excited, calm, anxious, or something else? When you actually began the process, how did your emotional state compare to those expectations?

If it helps to think through it, can you tell me:
- Were there particular moments or surprises that shifted how you felt?
- Did your feelings change over time or across different attempts?
- How did your partner, family, or past experiences influence your emotional readiness?
- What, if anything, helped you cope with emotions that were different from what you expected?

I figured I’d be excited, like the first time. Calm enough. Thought I could do it — plain and simple. Didn’t expect all the worry.

When it actually happened I was more nervous than I thought I’d be. Out here, being far from town makes you skittish. The whole time I kept thinking about what’d happen if something went wrong and the hospital was two hours away.

- Moments that shifted me: that ER visit for the preeclampsia did it. One minute I’m thinking “we’ll be fine,” next minute I’m in the ER and everything feels real and thin. Also when the traveling OB didn’t show up one month and the satellite was acting up for a tele-visit — that made me mad and scared both. Seeing the baby on an ultrasound calmed me some, but I didn’t get to see them as often as I’d wanted.

- Feelings over time: first baby I was more wide-eyed. Second I was busy and tired, not dreamy. By the third I was practical and a little worn down. Each baby’s different. This one had more worry because of health stuff and the patchwork care I had.

- Partner/family: My husband’s steady. He don’t fuss — just does the work. That helped. But family’s far away, so there wasn’t much help. Neighbors pitch in sometimes. Past births made me less scared of labor, but the medical scare made me wary.

- What helped: Keepin’ busy with chores, talking to a neighbor, praying, and plain stubbornness. Focusing on what had to get done. Telehealth helped when it worked, but most days you just do the next thing. Doesn’t feel like I got the “ready” people talk about, but I managed. 

If you want fancy feelings, I don’t got ’em. Out here you either do it or you don’t.

They didn't stick around. Folks at the clinic were in and out, said things quick like a list and gone. Nurse on the tele-visit told me to "call if you need" — the satellite drops out half the time, so that don't mean much out here.

One clear time: when I ended up in the ER with that high‑blood‑pressure scare, I sat in a room for hours. They checked me and left a note for follow‑up, but nobody sat with me and said what to expect. My husband stayed back on the ranch to mind the cattle — he called once, but couldn't come. Felt like I was holding the baby by myself that night and watching my phone for answers that didn't come.

Another thing: after the baby came, nobody came by to help me with the house or the older kids. Around here there ain't no drop‑in mother groups, so when I needed someone to say "you're doing okay" or help with a load of laundry, there was nobody. Simple stuff — someone to stay a couple hours, explain things plain, or bring a meal — that would've helped a lot.

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect you'd find when you looked online or used apps for fertility and getting pregnant? And when you actually searched, what did you find — did it give you what you needed?

If it helps to answer, tell me:
- Where did you look (websites, apps, social media, forums, friends)?
- What kinds of information did you expect (timing/ovulation, medical advice, lifestyle changes, emotional support)?
- Were the sources you found trustworthy and easy to understand?
- Did the information calm you or make you more worried/confused?
- Was anything important missing or hard to find?

I thought it'd be plain as day — tell me when to try, what to eat, when to see a doc. Figured the internet/apps would make it simple. That didn't happen.

Where I looked
- Phone apps that track cycles.  
- Google and sites folks mention — pregnancy places, health pages.  
- Facebook groups and a few forums.  
- Asked neighbors and cousins when I saw them.  
Out here we don't do a lot of online unless the satellite's working.

What I expected
- Clear timing/ovulation help so I knew when to try.  
- Simple medical advice and when to go to the hospital.  
- Plain lifestyle tips for a ranch woman — lifting, chemicals, work stuff.  
- Maybe some folks to talk to when it got scary.

What I actually found
- A lot of generic cycle trackers. They assume steady 28-day cycles and regular lives. That ain't how things go on a ranch.  
- Mixed advice everywhere. One site says one thing, another says the opposite.  
- Facebook groups had stories and pity and lots of scare posts. Hard to tell what's true.  
- Real medical answers were spotty — sometimes a telehealth NP, sometimes a traveling OB. No single record to lean on.

Trustworthiness and clarity
- Hard to trust. Too many ads, too many “experts” with big words. I like straight talk.  
- The medical pages were better but they didn't take into account how far I am from a hospital.  
- The telehealth stuff only works when the satellite does. When it doesn't, I got nothing.

How it made me feel
- More worried and confused most of the time. Conflicting answers just made me second-guess.  
- Friends and neighbors calmed me more than sites did. Real people who know the distance to the hospital.

What was missing or hard to find
- Practical plans for folks who live far from hospitals — when to leave, what signs mean "go now," how to transfer records.  
- Plain, no-nonsense guidance for heavy labor on the ranch and chemical exposures.  
- A way to keep one consistent prenatal record when you see different providers.  
- Offline or low-bandwidth resources. Most apps assume constant internet — doesn't apply out here.

Bottom line: apps are fine if you live in town. For me, they were mostly noise. I wanted clear, practical, local-minded answers and I didn't find them.

You mentioned you expected straightforward guidance from apps and sites but didn’t find it. What was the biggest gap between what digital sources told you and what your healthcare provider told you? Can you give an example, and did that difference change how much you trusted either source?

Biggest gap was plainness and how fast things needed doing. Apps and sites gave real tidy, general tips. My providers told me what actually mattered for me.

Example: an app said swelling and headaches in pregnancy can be normal, try rest and water. When my head hurt and my ankles blew up, the ER checked my pressure and said it was high — I had to get checked right then. That wasn’t the “try this at home” tone the app had.

Did it change trust? For anything that felt serious I trusted the person with the cuff and the paper chart more than a site. For general, boring stuff I still looked at apps, but out here lots of advice “drive to your clinic” doesn’t apply — nearest hospital’s 90 miles — so I take the apps with a grain of salt.

Did you have any pre-existing health condition before you got pregnant (for example diabetes, high blood pressure, thyroid issues, mental health conditions, an autoimmune disease, etc.)? If not, imagine you did — what would you have expected the healthcare system to do to help you get ready?

And when you actually sought help (or if you tried to), what happened instead?

A few quick things that might help your answer:
- Who did you expect to see — a GP, OB/GYN, specialist, or nurse?
- Did you expect medication review or changes, tests, or referrals?
- Did you expect clear, practical advice and follow-up?
- When you asked for help, were meds adjusted, were you referred, was information clear, was care coordinated, and did you feel supported?
Tell me as much as you remember — or give an example of one visit or conversation if that’s easier.

No. I didn't have any long‑term sickness before I got pregnant.

If I had, I'd have figured they'd put me with a real OB or a specialist, not just whoever rolled through once a month. I'd expect:
- someone to look at my meds and tell me what to stop or change,
- blood work and blood‑pressure checks more than once a month,
- a clear plan if things go wrong and somebody to call if I got worse,
- a referral to the big hospital if it was serious, and somebody to keep records so I didn't have to repeat everything.
Out here that feels normal to expect — a simple plan and someone who actually follows up. If you mean "who" — OB or a high‑risk doc and a nurse who could teach me practical stuff. Tests, meds review, and clear advice, yes.

What actually happened: scattered. The traveling OB came once a month. The telehealth nurse practitioner only when the satellite worked. I went to the ER once when my blood pressure went bad. Nobody had my full records because every place wrote things different and didn't share. Meds weren’t managed the way I expected. Instructions were loose.

Example: ER visit for high blood pressure.
- I had bad headaches and my pressure was high. I drove to the ER — 40 miles to the nearest small hospital. They gave me medicine, watched me a few hours, and said I needed to follow up with my OB.
- They sent me home that day. They told me what they did, but those notes didn't make it to the county clinic right away. When the traveling OB saw me later, they didn't have all the ER info and had to redo tests.
- Nobody called me the next day to check. The telehealth NP tried to help once the satellite connected, but the call cut out and I couldn't get a steady plan.
- So meds got changed here and there, but it wasn't coordinated. Felt like I was stitching things together myself.

Bottom line: if I had a pre‑existing thing, I'd want steady local care and somebody to keep my papers straight. Out here, that doesn't just happen.

Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you? Or was it somewhere in between?

Can you give an example — for instance, did you have to push for referrals, blood work, or medication review, or did providers contact you and explain next steps? If you did have to push, what helped you get what you needed, and what got in the way?

Mostly I had to push. The system didn’t exactly come looking for me out here.

Example: my blood pressure kept acting up. I had to badger the clinic for blood work and to get somebody to look at my meds. They sent me to the ER once after I kept calling and saying it was high. The traveling OB only shows up once a month, and the tele‑NP only when the satellite’s working — most of the time that doesn’t apply out here.

What helped: showing up in person, keeping a little log of my BP, my husband driving me when I couldn’t get an appointment, and not letting them skate by with “we’ll call you.” What got in the way: no steady doctor, spotty internet, long drives to town, and nobody having my records — every new person starts from scratch. If you don’t keep after them, nothing happens.

Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you imagined?

If it helps, tell me about specific things you expected or wanted them to do — for example, tracking cycles or using apps with you, coming to clinic visits, doing research, timing sex, changing diet or drinking/smoking habits, getting tests done, or just being emotionally supportive — and which of those they actually did or didn’t. 

Follow-ups I might ask depending on your answer:
- Were there any surprises — either good or bad — about how they showed up?
- Did their level of involvement change over time? Why do you think that was?
- Were there any disagreements about roles or decisions? How did you handle them?
- How did their involvement (or lack of it) affect you emotionally or practical decisions you made?

I figured he'd help like any normal husband: drive me to the clinic when I could get a ride, be there for the big appointments, help cut back on his beer if that mattered, and just... be steady. Maybe keep track of when I was due, call the nurse if I needed him to. I didn't think he'd be doin' phone apps or readin' medical papers with me. Out here that sort of stuff don't mean much.

What actually happened: he mostly did the practical stuff. Drove when I needed him to — he drove me to the ER that time my blood pressure went up — and covered the ranch chores so I could rest. He didn't come to the monthly clinic visits much; they're a long trip and he can't leave the place for days. He never used apps or tracked cycles. He didn't go lookin' up tests or switch his drinking much. Emotional support was hit-or-miss — steady when things got real, quiet the rest of the time.

Surprises: good — he was calmer than I thought when it was serious and got me to the ER without fuss. Bad — he wasn't much help with the paperwork and follow-up calls. That part I had to handle.

His involvement picked up near the end and in the emergency. Before that, it was mostly ranch work first. We argued once about when to leave for care — he wanted to wait till morning; I said we were going. I just made the call and he went along.

How it affected me: I felt on my own for the medical stuff. Stressed, yeah. But out here you get used to running things yourself. He helped where it mattered for the ranch and for getting me there when it counted. The rest didn't surprise me much.

Thanks — that’s helpful. A couple quick, gentle questions to get at that more: 

- Were there specific moments when you felt the preparation burden was falling mostly on you? Can you give an example (appointments, phone calls, researching stuff, organizing things at home)?  
- What do you think drove that — his work schedule, assumptions about roles, you needing to protect the pregnancy, or something else?  
- How did those moments make you feel, and did you talk to him about it? If so, how did he respond?  
- You started to say “I didn’t think he’d be doin’ phone…” — what did you mean by that?

Take your time — any detail helps. If it’s easier, tell one specific recent instance and we can go from there.

Yeah. Short answers.

- Yep. Mostly fell on me. One clear time was the ER trip for the preeclampsia signs. I was the one callin’ the clinic, callin’ around for a ride, gettin’ the kids sorted, packin’ a bag, makin’ the drive 90 miles. The traveling OB wasn’t in town. The telehealth NP wouldn’t connect ’cause the satellite kept blinkin’ out. I had to pull together whatever papers they asked for — there wasn’t no single chart. He stayed home to watch the cows and fix a gate.

- Why it fell on me: his work. When you run cattle you can’t just leave when the herd needs you. Also he don’t do that kind of stuff — phone calls, paperwork. Around here people expect the woman to handle the doctor-end of things. And I felt like I had to protect the baby, so I pushed it myself.

- How it made me feel: worried and ticked off. Kinda alone. I told him I needed help — told him straight he needed to at least call the clinic if he couldn’t come. He mostly said he couldn’t leave the herd or “do what I gotta do.” Sometimes he apologizes. Mostly he helps after the fact with chores, but he don’t take on the phone stuff.

- “I didn’t think he’d be doin’ phone…” I meant he ain’t the phone-callin’ type. He won’t sit on hold with a nurse or try to fiddle with telehealth when the internet’s bad. He can do the heavy work, but not the sittin’ around and organizin’ paperwork and appointments. Out here, that’s what I ended up doin’.

If you want the ER day laid out step-by-step, I can tell that plain.

I’m sorry if this is a difficult question — take your time. If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

You can tell me about things like:
- medical support you expected (early scans, blood tests, referrals to a specialist, investigations into cause, medication or supplements, a specific care plan)
- emotional support you expected (bereavement counselling, mental-health services, peer support groups)
- practical or system supports you expected (faster access to appointments, continuity of carer, clear points of contact, time off or workplace notes)
- who actually helped you (GP, midwife, OB, nurse, counsellor) and whether it felt timely and sympathetic
- any barriers you faced (long waits, cost, being dismissed or not taken seriously, language/cultural issues)
- how your partner was involved or supported

If you haven’t had a pregnancy loss, you can imagine what you would expect and what you'd want from the health service. Anything else you want me to ask about or probe further?

I did lose one, yes—between my second and third. Short answer: I expected more than I got.

- What I expected: early scan to make sure heart was beating, blood tests to see if something was wrong, maybe a referral to someone who actually sees you more than once a month, a plan so I wouldn't be left guessing. Some counseling would’ve been nice too.  
- What I actually got: monthly OB fly-in visits or the tele‑NP if the satellite worked. If something urgent, I had to go to the ER 90 miles away. No steady follow‑up, no real investigations. Mostly paperwork and being told to come back next clinic day.  
- Emotional help: I thought there’d be bereavement talk or someone to call. Nothing local. No groups. Counselling meant driving and taking off days — which out here don’t really exist.  
- Who helped: the county clinic nurse and the ER doctor did what they could. The traveling OB was fine when they were here, but only here once a month. The tele‑NP tried but the internet cut out more than it worked. Felt practical, not sympathetic.  
- Barriers: distance, long waits between providers, no continuous record to hand off between folks, cost/time to travel, and sometimes being brushed off like it was no big deal. Satellite telehealth is hit or miss.  
- Partner: he’s on the ranch full time. He helped with chores and the kids. Didn’t get any formal support either.  
- Workplace notes/time off: doesn’t apply out here — we don’t punch a clock, we just don’t have the spare hands.

You can ask about how the travel and spotty telehealth made follow‑ups fail, or what would help to keep records joined up between providers.

I'm sorry you went through that loss.

Was there a point where you felt your previous loss was not being taken seriously enough in the context of your new pregnancy attempt?

If you're comfortable, can you tell me more about that—when you felt it, who you felt didn't take it seriously, or what they said or did? Did that affect the care you were offered or the decisions you made? What would have made you feel more heard or supported?

Yeah. I told them I’d had a loss before. It felt like nobody much cared.

Mostly when the traveling OB came once a month. He’d listen five minutes, flip through my little chart like it was a grocery list, then say “we’ll watch” and leave. The tele‑NP—good luck getting her on the line when the satellite’s playing games. When I finally got through she acted like it was a fresh start, didn’t read the notes, asked the same questions I’d already answered. ER folks fixed what they needed to fix that day but they weren’t keeping up with the whole picture.

They sorta shrugged it off. “These things happen,” they said. That’s how it felt. It changed what I did — I didn’t push for extra tests or drive two days to the city for more care. Too far, too much hassle, and looking like I was making a fuss when they acted like it wasn’t a big deal. So I rode it out here at the clinic and on the ranch.

What would’ve helped? One person who knew my whole story and stayed on it. Someone to listen without cutting me off. An early ultrasound, earlier follow‑ups, and records that actually moved with me instead of disappearing between visits. Not a support group—don’t apply out here—but a real clinician who showed up or at least called back when they said they would. Simple as that.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

If it helps, you can tell me things like:
- Did you expect an early scan or blood tests? Reassurance about the previous loss?
- Who did you think would be there (midwife, GP, obstetrician, partner)?
- What did you expect the waiting room/clinic to feel like — calm, private, friendly?
- Did you expect clear explanations, a written plan, or follow-up appointments?
- How long were the visits, and did you feel listened to and supported?

Take your time — any detail is useful.

I figured it’d be simple. Early scan, some blood taken, someone telling me what to expect and when to come back. Thought there’d be a real person in the room most times — a midwife or doc — maybe my husband there. Thought the clinic’d be private and calm. Thought they’d give me a paper with a plan. That’s what I expected.

Doesn’t work like that out here.

First months it was waiting for the traveling OB. He shows up once a month. The rest I dealt with a telehealth nurse if the satellite worked that day. Half the time the internet didn’t, so the call was dropped or I couldn’t get through. I saw ER folks one time when my blood pressure acted up. No steady person. No steady record. They ask the same questions every visit like they ain’t got my notes.

I did expect an early ultrasound. We waited a long time for that — until the OB came through. Blood tests happened at the county clinic but not all at once. Sometimes they’d send me off for labs to the hospital 90 miles away. That drive’s a whole day out here, so it’s a big deal.

Waiting room wasn’t calm or private. It’s the little clinic — other sick folks, kids, folks in coveralls. Not much hush. Sometimes the nurse was nice. Mostly it felt rushed. Visits were short. Ten, maybe fifteen minutes most times. Telehealth calls shorter. I felt half-listened to. They’d tell me things quick, sometimes in words I don’t follow, and then I’m left wondering what to do next.

No real written plan. Mostly verbal: “come back next month,” or “keep your eye on your blood pressure.” When I had preeclampsia signs, that’s when they actually sent me to the ER and took it serious. But before that, it was patchwork care — this doc, that nurse, one ER visit. My husband couldn’t always come because of the ranch work. So a lot fell on me.

So yeah — I expected more steady care, early scans, clear follow-up. What I got was spotty visits, bad internet, lots of travel, and nobody keeping a consistent chart. Out here, you learn to make do.

Thanks — what was the biggest surprise, positive or negative, about those first appointments?

If it helps, tell me one thing that pleasantly surprised you and one thing that let you down. A few things you might mention: did staff listen to you and explain things clearly? Did you feel respected and given privacy? Were you allowed to have your husband there? Did anything happen that made you trust or distrust the clinic?

Pleasant surprise — when the traveling doc actually showed up he sat down and listened. Didn’t feel rushed for once, and he let me ask things plain.

Let down — nobody kept my records straight. Three different folks telling me three different things, telehealth kept dropping out, and the ER people brushed off my blood‑pressure till it got bad. That made me real wary.

My husband was allowed at visits and the hospital — that was fine. Privacy? Clinic’s small, thin walls — not much. Out here you got to make do.

Thanks — and take your time. Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like? For example:

- Who did you think would explain the results (midwife, doctor, sonographer)?
- Would you expect it in person, over the phone, or in writing — and how soon after the test?
- Did you expect explanations of what the numbers/risks meant, next steps, and written information to take away?
- Did you expect emotional support or a chance to ask questions?

And what actually happened? If it helps, tell me about any moments that felt particularly clear or particularly confusing.

I figured the traveling OB or the clinic nurse would tell me. Maybe the tech who did the scan if they were there. I thought it’d be in person at the clinic, same day or at the next visit. I expected plain talk — what the numbers meant, what to do next — and a bit of paper to take home. I wanted to be able to ask questions and someone to say, plain, “this is okay” or “this isn’t.”

What actually happened was messy. The OB only comes once a month. Most of the time I got a phone call if the satellite behaved. The telehealth NP would leave numbers or a message and I’d be like, “okay… now what?” No paper most times. The ER was the only place that was straight with me — when they told me I had preeclampsia and needed to be watched, that was clear and quick. Scans at the big hospital felt rushed; the tech didn’t explain the pictures. Lots of “we’ll monitor” and no one saying how or when. I never got a good, written summary to keep. Felt like I was chasing pieces of my own pregnancy.

Particularly clear: ER doc telling me to come in for my blood pressure — short and direct. Particularly confusing: telehealth message with numbers I don’t understand and no one to explain them in plain words. Out here, that kind of back-and-forth doesn’t work well.

Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

If yes: what specific information did you feel was missing (numbers, what they meant for the baby, next steps, written materials)? Did you feel able to ask for more explanation, and from whom? How did that affect how much control you felt you had over decisions about your care?

Yes.

Missing bits. They'd tell me "your numbers are high" but not the numbers. I wanted to know the exact blood‑pressure numbers, what that meant for the baby, and what signs to watch for at home. No clear next steps written down. No paper I could read later — just someone saying things during a quick visit and gone. I never got a simple list: when to come in, what medicines do what, who to call. That kind of plain, written stuff.

I asked when I could. Sometimes I tried on the telehealth calls, but the satellite would drop, so I couldn't finish. The traveling OB was only here once a month — short visit, lots to cover. The ER folks were busy and curt. So I felt I couldn't really get more explanation when I needed it.

It left me feeling like I was just following orders, not deciding. Out here, ninety miles to the hospital, that matters — I needed clear reasons to leave the ranch. Without numbers or a written plan, I felt less control and had to guess a lot.
